display: In reviewing the cookbook and setup guide, visually these are great. But content wise they display a lack of comprehensive understanding of the tooling at your disposal. Here is what I see the benefit to be of cleo: the tui <- concurrently run bcp tasks and manage multi agent sessions in one single tui - also task/run because youre defining items of work that can be accomplished by an agent in an automated way - the history and rollback command, for quickly experimenting with agents and skills, I also want to make it so cleo is flexible enough to accept an arbitrary registry url and use that for agent/skill submission/installation/searching
I am seeing this at docs.bitcontextprotocol.com: [Pasted text #1 +5 lines]

display: We need to continue to work on our cookbook examples, even the setup guide could use work. For example, a lot of the spec/markdown in the examples is light weight and useless. For example review the spec that generated cleo @/home/nicks-dgx/dev/bundt/KIRO-CLI-HIRO-SPEC.md - Maybe it would benefit us to have a few example specs to support the cookbooks. The benefit is with bcp these specs can be lengthy and we can use bcp to annotate priority or code examples or content

In our last session, we released bcp/driver bcp/mcp bundt/cleo bundt/dxdocs bundt/prev and bundt signals. Then we set up aws infrastructure for both websites and documentation sites. Then we synced the bitcontextprotocol website and bitcontextprotocol docsite to s3 with aws cli. Now we are shoring up the bundt website and bundt docsite. Orient yourself on the current state of our work, and then when you have a comprehensive understanding, respond with 'OK'

I want you to very deeply study cleo and prev, I believe that these tools will comprise a good deal of our cookbook. In this session, I want you and I to brainstorm about some great recipes for this cookbook. Additionally, I want us to brainstorm some extremely cool projects that we can build with cleo, prev, signals, waavy, etc and I want to showcase the efficacy of this bun/ai-first toolkit. This will become our showcase and will be a part of our website (bundt-dev.io)

I think you have come up with some really great ideas. Im impressed. Here's a list of which directions excite me the most for the _cookbook_: 3. Prompt Engineering Lab 4. Cross-Project Agent Sharing 5. Agent-Composed Dashboard 8. Full-Stack AI App: Support Dashboard ; Additionally, here are the directions that excite me regarding the showcase projects: 1. Prev Playground - What do you think we should start with/what the plan should be?
We would need to set that up. I think ec2 or digital ocean should be suitable. We can create the playground as a standalone deployable using docker

Prior to building this out, I'd like you to enter planning mode and generate a well thought out implementation plan using the knowledge you've gathered on prev servers and our goal in this Prev Playground showcase. I want to understand what we're going to be building beforehand so i can modify the plan or approve it

I like your implementation plan, it's good. I want to discuss the section: [Pasted text #1 +27 lines] 


 If we need this already for a showcase, it's likely our consumers would also. Instead of importing directly from private code paths, would it not make more sense to modify and re-deploy prev so we can define custom routes and middleware, as well as   - A polished public-facing HTML page (not the dev playground)
  - A /api/compose endpoint that bridges natural language → composition
  - Rate limiting for public access 


 The file plan is good you don't need to modify that at all. All fragments, data sources, and presets are good, same there, no need to modify anything there.  Composition Flow is also solid. Actually everything about this plan is fantastic, the docker file, build order, features, playground html, etc. I think the only thing we need to change is that we should not be indexing private routes from prev so anything we need in the app needs to either be exposed from prev or added to it functionality wise

Perfect, yes ready to start. After each step in the build order, update a working context document that can be used to pick up this scope of work with enough details to complete the remaining phases, and what the current state of the workspace is
Review @PREV-SERVER-IMPLEMENTATION-PLAN.md  and @.claude/specs/PLAYGROUND_BUILD.md - were in the middle of phase 2, orient yourself on the current state of the work and continue
Is it possible the "bun" field in the package.json is causing this

Continue with the prev workspace showcase work and the cookbook recipe work. When your context window hits 88%, stop and write a thorough context document and prompt document that supports picking back up this work in a subsequent session. Context documents should be extremely thorough as to not see a degradation in quality session over session

I have found myself wanting a log of our work session over session in this workspace. I have created a '.jsonl' file in .claude/custom/work-history.jsonl - whenever you output a summary (for example, you just output a summary) I want you to write an entry to this file containing a metadata json object that contains: chat-session-uuid timestamps, prompt summaries, summary content (exactly no paraphrasing), files edited, etc. I want you to update recent-chats.jsonl, prompt-history.jsonl, and work-history.jsonl in conjunction, session over session, message over message as bodies of work are accomplished/started/implemented over our sessions. I want you to update your memory for the agent 'token-minimalist' so that the above is retained session over session as long as Im using the token-minimalist agent
